articles.headline: Safety Culture in Aviation SMS
articles.blurb: How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.
articles.takeaway: SMS effectiveness depends on psychological safety that encourages frontline workers to report hazards without fear of punishment, shifting accountability from individuals to systems.

articles.headline: Carmel Ortiz on the evolving landscape of satellite communications

articles.takeaway: MEO satellite constellations are emerging as a cost-effective alternative to LEO for aviation connectivity, offering reduced latency and deployment complexity compared to traditional geostationary systems.

articles.headline: The Evolution of Aviation SMS
articles.blurb: From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.
articles.takeaway: Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically identifying and mitigating hazards before incidents develop.

articles.headline: 5 Reasons Why The Boeing 777X Needs Folding Wingtips

articles.headline: Why Seat Width & Pitch Vary So Much Between US Airlines
articles.blurb: No two airlines in the US are really quite the same, especially in terms of seats. What makes each airline so drastically different?
articles.takeaway: Airlines deliberately differentiate seat configurations as a competitive strategy to target specific market segments and maximize revenue per aircraft rather than standardizing for passenger comfort.

articles.headline: How Rolls-Royce Engines Differ From General Electric Engines
articles.blurb: Both engine manufacturers power some of the world's largest commercial jets.
articles.takeaway: Rolls-Royce engines typically prioritize fuel efficiency through advanced materials and geared architectures, while GE engines emphasize raw thrust and modular design flexibility across diverse aircraft platforms.

articles.headline: Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)
articles.blurb: Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...
articles.takeaway: Helicopter pilots systematically underestimate wire strike risk due to overconfidence in reaction time, making pre-flight route planning and terrain awareness the only reliable mitigation strategies.

articles.headline: Phantom Space buys thermal specialist to support orbital data center push

articles.takeaway: Premium airlines justify $10K+ fares by offering enclosed suites with doors, direct aisle access, and shower spas—features now expected rather than differentiated in ultra-luxury cabin competition.

articles.headline: Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization
articles.blurb: Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.
articles.takeaway: Operational optimization through integrated airline, airport, and airspace technologies offers a near-term path to accommodate growing demand while reducing aviation's environmental footprint.
keywords: aviation; optimization; operations research; sustainability; environmental impacts

articles.headline: First Airbus A220 Hull Loss: Maintenance Fire Takes Out airBaltic Jet
articles.blurb: The Airbus A220 has suffered its first known total loss, and the circumstances make it especially unusual. Rather than a crash or an in-service accident, the airBaltic aircraft, registered as YL-AAO, was lost due to a ground fire. The damage from the fire was later deemed beyond economic repair.
articles.takeaway: The A220's first hull loss occurred on the ground during maintenance, highlighting critical fire prevention protocols for newer aircraft types in hangar environments.

articles.headline: NASA Launches Artemis II, the First Crewed Lunar Flyby in 50 Years
articles.blurb: Artemis II is NASA’s first mission with crew aboard the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft, and the first beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972. After a first launch attempt was scrubbed in February 2026, the Artemis II mission launched from Kennedy Space Center’s Launch...
articles.takeaway: Artemis II's successful crewed lunar flyby validates the SLS-Orion architecture for deep space missions, establishing the operational foundation for sustained lunar exploration and future Mars missions.
